pipeline_results.packages_engine_vitest: FAIL 2/10 (crud list ordering + mermaid parser API)
pipeline_results.drift_detect_ts: FAIL exit=1 (expected — catalog vs Rust drift, pre-existing and flagged in G8-C commit)

findings.claim: Scaffolder template smoke test references APIs that do not exist in @benten/engine and contradict the wrapper's public Trace shape.
findings.detail: Two drifts inside the generated template's smoke.test.ts: (a) line 61 calls `crud('restricted', { capability: 'store:restricted:*' })` — `CrudOptions` in packages/engine/src/dsl.ts defines only `label` and `hlc`; there is no `capability` field. The generated project will typecheck-fail at `npm run test` (or silently be ignored by a loose type check), breaking Gate 3 (`cap_denial_routes_on_denied`). (b) line 77 asserts `(s.elapsedNs ?? 0) > 0` on trace steps, but `TraceStep` in packages/engine/src/types.ts exposes `durationUs`, not `elapsedNs`. Every step will read undefined and the `.some()` call returns false, breaking Gate 4 (`trace_non_zero_timing`). The bindings/napi and packages/engine surfaces both agree on `durationUs` — the template is the outlier.
findings.suggested_fix: Two edits in tools/create-benten-app/template/test/smoke.test.ts: (1) replace `capability: 'store:restricted:*'` with a registration path the DSL actually supports (either drop Gate 3 with a TODO hooked to a Phase-2 capability wrapper, or switch to calling `engine.grantCapability` + registering the same `crud('restricted')` handler and asserting a deny path — but both require a capability-aware crud variant that does not yet exist in B6). (2) replace `s.elapsedNs ?? 0` with `s.durationUs` to match the wrapper's `TraceStep`.

findings.claim: @mermaid-js/parser does not parse `flowchart` diagrams — the chosen test dep cannot validate the Mermaid output the wrapper produces.
findings.detail: Inspected node_modules/@mermaid-js/parser/dist/mermaid-parser.core.mjs lines 108-116: the exported `parse(diagramType, text)` registers initializers only for `info`, `packet`, `pie`, `architecture`, `gitGraph`, `radar`, `treemap`. Any other diagramType throws 'Unknown diagram type: flowchart'. That matches the observed vitest failure in this session: `src/mermaid.test.ts > mermaid_output_parses_as_flowchart — Error: Unknown diagram type: flowchart`. The scaffolder template's Gate 5 (`mermaid_output_parses`) will fail end-to-end against any user's machine for the same reason. `@mermaid-js/parser` is the wrong parser for flowcharts — flowchart parsing lives in the main `mermaid` package (or `@mermaid-js/mermaid` CLI). This is a silent test-infrastructure wrong-tool pick, not a renderer bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) use the `mermaid` package and call `mermaid.parse(source)` (which validates flowchart syntax by returning parseDiagram info, throws on invalid) in both mermaid.test.ts and the scaffolder template's smoke.test.ts; or (b) fall back to a regex structural check (starts with `/^flowchart /`, at least one `-->` edge, every edge target has a matching node declaration) until a flowchart-capable parser is wired. Option (a) is authoritative and matches the test's stated intent ('if @mermaid-js/parser accepts it, Mermaid renders it'); (b) is a pragmatic Phase-1 escape hatch. Update the scaffolder template's package.json dep at the same time.

findings.claim: `crud_post_zero_config_full_cycle` asserts insertion-order list output but the IVM content_listing view is ordered by the tx_id fallback when the engine's ChangeEvent carries identity-only payloads, which interleaves the expected order.
findings.detail: Observed failure output: `["second", "first", "third"]` expected `["first", "second", "third"]`. The bindings/napi test run logs the underlying cause: `benten-ivm: content_listing received identity-only event (no node, no createdAt); falling back to tx_id=... — this is a Phase-1 defense-in-depth path`. So the IVM view sees no createdAt on the ChangeEvent and falls back to tx_id for ordering — the DSL wrapper injects createdAt into the NODE's properties, but the change-event pipeline does not carry that createdAt through to IVM in the CRUD fast-path. This is an integration quirk between the wrapper's injector (engine.ts:270-283), the napi `registerCrud` fast path, and the G5/G7 change-stream plumbing. The symptom is Phase-1-observable via the TS wrapper's tests. It is not a critical blocker (`items` contents are correct, only ordering wrong) but it breaks one of the exit-criterion tests on the TS side.
findings.suggested_fix: Two options: (1) have engine.ts wrap `items` with a client-side sort by `createdAt` on `list` results when the CRUD fast path is in use — the wrapper already stamps createdAt so it can be used as the sort key; (2) push the createdAt injection down into `register_crud` in Rust so the ChangeEvent carries the Node identity AND the createdAt property, eliminating the tx_id fallback. (2) is the structurally correct fix (makes the defense-in-depth path truly a fallback) but is G7/G5 scope. (1) is a surgical G8-B fix — add 3-4 lines after the `if (crud && dispatchOp === 'list')` block to sort by `createdAt` when all items have it. Recommended for this mini-review cycle: take (1) now, file (2) for a G7 post-impl touch-up.

findings.claim: `revoke_capability(cid)` passes the CID string as the `actor` argument to the engine's revoke call — this writes a revocation record whose actor field is the grant CID, not the actor that was originally granted.
findings.detail: The implementation lists the compromise explicitly in its doc comment: 'In Phase-1 we pass the CID string as the actor to form a valid revocation record'. But the shape of the written revocation record diverges from what `grant_capability` wrote: the original grant carries `actor` = caller-supplied actor CID string; the revoke writes `actor` = the grant's CID. Anything downstream that joins revoke records against grant records by `actor` (Phase-3 capability-chain verification, audit trail reconstruction) will miss the connection. The TS wrapper surfaces `revokeCapability(cid)` as a terminal method, so consumers reasonably expect it to actually revoke the grant identified by that CID — not to write a stub record.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) remove `revoke_capability` from the public napi surface for Phase-1 (return `E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED` so callers learn explicitly), or (b) surface the grant's actor by reading the grant Node via `get_node(cid)` and extracting `properties.actor`, then pass THAT as the revoke's actor argument. (b) is only a few lines and makes the record semantically valid. The current path (CID-as-actor) is a silent correctness trap — it looks like it works but produces misaligned records.

findings.claim: `Engine.trace` synthesizes the result via a second `call()` when the native trace omits one — this doubles the evaluation for every trace and can drift the CID.
findings.detail: The comment acknowledges the issue: 'the parallel call is idempotent in shape but produces a separate Node (the content-address differs by createdAt)'. For the crud create path this means `trace(...)` evaluates the subgraph twice (once traced, once to fill in `.result`), creating two distinct content-addressed Nodes with different createdAt stamps. The side-effect surprise: `trace()` mutates engine state twice. For idempotent handlers this is merely wasteful; for non-idempotent writes it doubles the transaction count and inflates the change stream.
findings.suggested_fix: Preferred: extend the napi `trace` surface to include `result` in its return value (plumb the outcome from the traced invocation through, rather than discarding it). That eliminates the need for the synthesized call entirely. If that is out of scope for this touch-pass, at minimum guard the synthesis behind a check that the handler is read-only — dispatch `:get` / `:list` without the double-call risk, but avoid re-evaluating `:create` / `:write` paths.

findings.claim: `emit_event` is a silent no-op — callers who expect EMIT semantics (event stream notification) receive no indication that nothing happened.
findings.detail: The doc comment says 'Phase-1 is a no-op at the host level'. That is a defensible Phase-1 scope decision, but a silent no-op is a DX trap: callers test `engine.emitEvent(...)` in their handler code, get no error, and proceed believing events are being dispatched. They then find out at Phase-2 integration time that nothing ever fired.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) surface `E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED` explicitly so the caller knows EMIT is gated, or (b) route the event through the change stream as a synthetic write-marker Node so tests can observe the call actually did something. (a) matches the project's explicit-Phase-1 stance for the other four deferred primitives (WAIT/STREAM/SUBSCRIBE/SANDBOX — evaluator throws `E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED`), so consistency argues for (a).

findings.claim: `NativeEngine` interface declares every method optional — the TS wrapper tolerates a binding with any subset of the surface, which hides `napi build` drift rather than surfacing it.
findings.detail: Every field on `NativeEngine` carries a `?`, with a stated rationale: 'napi-rs generates signatures we cannot strictly audit at compile time'. This means if `napi build` produces a binding missing, e.g., `registerCrud` (napi-rs macro drop, feature-flag mishap, partial rebuild), the wrapper's runtime guards (`if (!this.inner.call) throw ...`) surface a runtime `E_DSL_INVALID_SHAPE`. That is a late failure mode; ideally the wrapper would fail at `Engine.open()` by auditing the method inventory once and reporting all missing methods at once.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single `validateBinding(native)` call inside `loadNative()` (or `Engine.open`) that asserts the methods the Phase-1 contract requires (create_node/get_node/update_node/delete_node/register_subgraph/register_crud/call/trace/handler_to_mermaid/create_view/read_view/grant_capability/revoke_capability/count_nodes_with_label/change_event_count/ivm_subscriber_count). Throw `E_DSL_INVALID_SHAPE` with the full missing-method list. This makes the failure mode load-time + actionable rather than call-site-surprise.

findings.claim: `Engine.close()` is effectively a no-op (sets a flag) — consumers that await `close()` in tests expecting filesystem release (e.g. before `rmSync(tmp)`) may race with redb's lock.
findings.detail: `close()` only sets `this.closed = true`. The napi Engine class holds an `Arc<InnerEngine>` that is only dropped when JS GC reclaims the wrapper. Tests that do `await engine.close(); rmSync(tmp, {recursive: true})` may race on Windows / some Linux kernels where the redb file lock survives until GC. The scaffolder template's smoke.test.ts does exactly this pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Expose an explicit `close()` method on the napi Engine that calls `Arc::try_unwrap(self.inner)` (or uses a mutex+Option<InnerEngine> to allow explicit drop) and release the redb file handle. Phase-1 could also accept the weaker contract of 'close() hints GC should run' and at least call `global.gc?.()` if available — but that requires `--expose-gc` and is flaky. The principled fix is a napi-side `close()` method.

findings.claim: `"type": "module"` on a package whose primary export is the CJS napi-rs loader is a shape mismatch acknowledged by engine.ts but propagates to bindings/napi/index.test.ts.
findings.detail: engine.ts:91-99 explicitly calls out this mismatch: 'G8-A's bindings/napi/package.json declares "type": "module", which causes the napi-rs-generated CJS index.js to fail under ESM loading'. The workaround (try platform-specific `.node` filename first, fall back to `index.js`) works, but it means every Node consumer that tries a plain `require('@benten/engine-native')` or a default `import` of the package entry point hits the CJS-in-ESM trap. `bindings/napi/index.test.ts` uses `createRequire(import.meta.url)` to work around it in-process; external consumers won't have that convenience.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `"type": "module"` from bindings/napi/package.json (the package exports a .node addon via CJS — ESM type is wrong). Then re-run `napi build` to regenerate the `index.js` dispatcher; the package will load cleanly via both CJS `require` and ESM `import` (ESM default-imports of CJS packages work). Update engine.ts's loadNative() to drop the platform-triplet hack since the standard `index.js` dispatcher will work.

findings.claim: Catalog `extractBullet` regex is non-greedy with `/s` flag but assumes the next bullet or blank line ends the value — multi-line fix hints that wrap with indented continuation lines will be silently truncated.
findings.detail:
The regex `/\-\s+\*\*${label}:?\*\*\s+(.+?)(?=
\-\s+\*\*|

|$)/s` terminates on a blank line OR the next top-level bullet. If the catalog author writes a fix hint that contains an indented code block followed by more prose (common for hints that include `cargo nextest run ...` + explanation), the indented line is preserved but a subsequent blank line terminates early. This is silent truncation — no error, just a shorter `fixHint` than authored.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the blank-line terminator with an explicit next-section terminator: terminate on `
-\s+\*\*` (next bullet, same section) or `
##\s+` (next header) or `
###\s+` (next code section), NOT on `

`. Add a CI check that codegen round-trips are idempotent (run codegen twice, diff should be empty — already enforced by the `verify up to date` step in ci.yml:278-282, so only the truncation correctness matters).

findings.claim: Two drift-detector scripts coexist (drift-detect.ts and drift-detect-error-catalog.mjs) enforcing the same contract — divergence risk between them is not monitored.
findings.detail: CLAUDE.md + the file headers call these 'both enforce the same contract and either one failing is a merge blocker'. But there is no cross-check that the two scripts implement the same parsing logic. Nothing prevents a maintainer from updating one and not the other, leaving a PR green by one detector and red by the other depending on which CI workflow runs first.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete `drift-detect-error-catalog.mjs` — `drift-detect.ts` is strictly newer and more comprehensive. Any legacy invocation paths should be redirected via a shim (e.g. a one-line wrapper `.mjs` that `import`s the TS version via tsx). A single source of truth is easier to keep correct than two parallel implementations.

findings.claim: Drift detector currently fails — CI will block every PR until the catalog/Rust enum discrepancy is reconciled.
findings.detail: Local reproduction confirmed: `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/drift-detect.ts; echo $?` returns 1. 10 catalog codes missing from Rust (E_DSL_*, E_INV_SANDBOX_NESTED, E_INV_SYSTEM_ZONE, three E_SANDBOX_*, three E_SYNC_*) and 8 extra Rust codes missing from the catalog (E_BACKEND_NOT_FOUND, E_CID_*, E_NOT_FOUND, E_VALUE_FLOAT_*, E_VERSION_BRANCHED). The G8-C commit message and plan acknowledge this ('currently failing due to pre-existing catalog↔Rust drift'), but leaving the CI gate red means the drift-detector job blocks every PR from merging until reconciled. This is a scheduling issue (the gate was added before the reconciliation PR landed), not a detector bug. Acceptable to ship red temporarily only if a follow-up PR is promised soon.
findings.suggested_fix: Land a follow-up PR within one day that either (a) updates docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md to add the 8 missing Rust codes and reconciles naming for the 10 missing-in-Rust codes; or (b) updates crates/benten-core/src/error_code.rs to match the catalog exactly. Preferred: (a) because the catalog is the source of truth per ARCHITECTURE.md — Rust should follow. Do not land more PRs on main with the drift-detector red; it masks future drifts.

findings.claim: `handlerPredecessors` returns an empty adjacency map unconditionally — the trace topological-order check in trace.test.ts is a no-op.
findings.detail: `handlerPredecessors(_handlerId)` always returns `{ predecessorsOf: () => [] }`. The comment says 'when the native binding exposes predecessor-table read, swap in this.inner.handlerPredecessors(_)'. Meanwhile, trace.test.ts:41-47 uses this adjacency table to validate topological order — with an empty table, the validation passes trivially (no predecessors to check). Tests that should fail on a broken topological-ordering evaluator pass silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a napi surface that exposes the handler's predecessor table (engine.rs should already have this internally for invariant validation), or (b) have the TS wrapper reconstruct the predecessor table from the `Subgraph.nodes[].edges` field stored on the `RegisteredHandler`. (b) is pure-TS, a few lines, and would give trace.test.ts real teeth without new napi surface area.

findings.claim: `json_to_subgraph_spec` silently ignores primitive kinds other than `write` and `respond` — a DSL payload with READ/TRANSFORM/BRANCH/ITERATE/CALL/EMIT primitives errors with 'unsupported kind'.
findings.detail: The function explicitly returns `InvalidArg` for kinds other than 'write'/'respond'. This means TS callers using SubgraphBuilder to build anything beyond trivial write+respond chains get an immediate `E_DSL_INVALID_SHAPE`-shaped error from the napi side when they call `registerSubgraph`. The wrapper in engine.ts recognizes the CRUD fast path (`registerCrud`) but for hand-built subgraphs the napi side only supports two primitives. The DSL `crud('post')` output includes BRANCH+READ+WRITE nodes — it relies on the `registerCrud` fast path to sidestep this limitation; anything hand-built in SubgraphBuilder will blow up.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) extend `json_to_subgraph_spec` to parse all 8 executable primitives, matching the evaluator's execution surface; (b) fail loudly with a clear `E_PRIMITIVE_NOT_IMPLEMENTED` that mentions Phase-1 registration supports the CRUD fast path only; (c) document the limitation prominently in packages/engine/README or QUICKSTART.md. (a) is the proper fix — the evaluator already handles READ/WRITE/TRANSFORM/RESPOND/BRANCH/ITERATE/CALL/EMIT per the G6 commits, so wiring them up in json_to_subgraph_spec is consistent work; the napi-side blind spot is just not wired. Until then, any documentation suggesting users call `engine.registerSubgraph(hand-built)` is misleading.

findings.claim: `__nodeCounter` is a module-level mutable integer — two concurrent SubgraphBuilders share state, producing node-id collisions across parallel-used builders.
findings.detail: `nextNodeId` increments a module-global counter. If two callers build subgraphs in parallel (likely in any test suite or multi-handler registration path), the ids interleave — builder A gets `read-1`, builder B gets `write-2`, builder A gets `respond-3`, etc. Since each builder only checks `this.lastId` within its own node list, the sequence is still locally valid, but the ids are confusing and — worse — a CaseBuilder sharing the same counter makes the case nodes' ids dependent on every other builder that happened to run before it. For content-addressing, local IDs are not part of the hash, so this is cosmetic. For debug/diagnostics (Mermaid labels, trace output), it is noisy.
findings.suggested_fix: Scope the counter per builder: move `__nodeCounter` into a `SubgraphBuilder` instance field, and pass it down to `CaseBuilder` via the constructor. This also makes subgraph building deterministic in parallel tests.

notes: G8 whole-group review — accept-with-minors. No critical findings. Four majors: two scaffolder template bugs that will fail exit-criterion gates on cold-install (g8-cr-1), one test-infrastructure wrong-tool pick that prevents flowchart validation (g8-cr-2), one IVM/wrapper integration quirk breaking list ordering (g8-cr-3), and one correctness issue in revoke_capability (g8-cr-4). Eleven minors covering binding-surface validation, close-semantics, ESM/CJS type declaration, trace double-eval, drift-detector duplication, etc. Pipeline state: Rust side is clean (fmt/clippy/check/doc/wasm all green); TS typecheck clean; bindings/napi 7/7 pass; packages/engine 8/10 with the two failures traceable to g8-cr-2 and g8-cr-3. The G8-B handoff memo's list of 'integration quirks' (type:module mismatch, subgraph payload understanding, trace result shape, list flattening, emit_event no-op) is largely reflected in findings g8-cr-3/5/6/9/14 — those are legitimate fix-pass items, not minor polish. Recommend a G8 fix-pass that addresses g8-cr-1, g8-cr-2, g8-cr-3 at minimum before calling the group done — the scaffolder template cannot be advertised as the 10-minute DX path while two of its six exit gates fail against the shipped wrapper.
